title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Andrew Ng: Opening Keynote
text:
New York's very special. Many of you

and um what I want to do today is chat

was work done by uh MER his organization
that studies the length of task that AI
could do successfully with at least a
50% success rate so x-axis is model
release date. Y-axis is how long it
takes a human to do a task. So sometimes
it take me you know a second or 15
seconds or a minute or four minutes and
so on to do. And according to their
analysis the length of task meaning how
long does a human take to do this um is
doubling every seven months. And so this
rate of progress is s and this is even
without agentic workflows which I think
will even this further. Um, and for AI
coding, the doubling time, you know,
code that used to take me a minute, two

time has ever been to build with AI. Um,
and I think the two things are more

how to use and also faster software

and in terms of what I see in building

bucket my work into two categories.
Sometimes um I'm building a quick and
dirty prototype you know just to test an
idea and sometimes I'm writing or
maintaining scalable production software
and it's hard to get very rigorous
studies on what exactly is the
acceleration part because that field is
moving so fast but depending on which

scalable software is like 50% faster
that feels you know maybe feels right to

it feels more like 10x or maybe even
much more than 10x because when you're

testing only on your laptop say the

for security reliability all those are
much lower and so um I'm seeing many

want to innovate let's build 20

know that some teams are lamenting that
a lot of AI proof concepts don't make it
into production. I actually feel
differently. Um instead if if the price
of finding two great ideas to take the
scale is you have to build 18 other
things, you know, to die a quick quiet
death. That's okay. I would rather focus
on driving the cost of those 18 proof of
concepts towards zero. And this is
becoming a new mechanism for teams to
invent.
I know that in Silicon Valley and around

because you know it broke stuff. Uh
okay, now you can take a picture. But
[laughter]
um but to to me I think some teams took
away from that that you shouldn't move
fast. But I think that's a mistake. I
think a lot of the most responsible
teams actually move really fast. We just
test out software in sandbox safe
environments to see what's wrong, figure
out what's wrong before we then let out
into the broader world and I see really
effective really fastm moving teams.
One of the most exciting things or most
interesting things to me has been
watching the bottlenecks in software
development shift. So when um at DBI or
AI fun um we build software, we often go
through this loop where we write
software, we build a prototype and then
go and get user feedback. We call this
the PM or product management [snorts]
where then based on learnings we go back
and improve the software and we go
around this loop many times to try to
build a product that users love and so
building software is becoming much
faster tools like codeex maybe Gemini
CLI really accelerating development with
a few we also hear from Versel Trey many
of these tools are accelerating the rate
ocean we should build software and the
fascinating thing is the bottleneck is
increasingly getting user feedback um on
a lot of my teams, we actually taught
everyone to code. Not just a software
engineer, but a front desk receptionist,
my CFO, my recruiter, everyone codes.
And remember, you know, a year ago, if a
recruiter writes code, everyone's like,
"Wow, you wrote a software. That's so
exciting." Now, if one of my team
members writes code, it's like, "Okay,
that's nice." It's become a part of our
daily workflow and um getting feedback
to improve your product is becoming a
bigger bottleneck. So some fascinating
trends I'm seeing um historically the
engineer to PM ratio would talk about
like a 4:1 or 7 to1 ratio. One PM can
keep you know like four to eight or
whatever engineers busy and take these
ratios with a grain of salt. But with
product management becoming a bigger
bottleneck. I'm seeing teams have this
ratio trend down maybe even two or one
to one which is kind of weird. One PM
and one engineer working together. It's
kind of weird but I'm seeing some teams
trend in that direction. And the other
I'm seeing is um I feel I have a you
know engineing development background
but I find that engineers that also
learn to do some product work can then
frankly be a team of one and drive
incredibly fast progress. You ship
software get feedback build software get
feedback. I remember a couple weekends
ago hang with my kids by a kids game. It
took me literally 10 minutes. I timed
it. took me 10 minutes with quad code to
write it and then it took me about an
hour to track down you know some of my
daughter's friends to get feedback right
from so so the PM work is actually much
slower than um than than than the
prototyping
and with all of this excitement and
stuff we can do um I just wrap with uh
notes on the broader national sentiment
on AI here in you know New York and
Silicon Valley I feel lucky to live in
almost a little bit of a bubble
surrounded by development friends all
excited about the acceleration and um I
pay a lot of attention to the fact that
unfortunately AI has not yet won
America's hearts and minds um this data
from a couple pure research studies one
released less than a month ago one a few
weeks before that but in this diagram
red is more concerned and excited about
AI green is more excited and concerned
so green is good red is you know less
good for AI so in 2021 37 7% were more
concerned and excited. 18% were more
excited and concerned 2022. And then
right after the launch of CH GPT, a lot
more people became more concerned about
AI. And this is partly because a number
of tech companies ran a massive PR
campaign to tell people don't trust AI.
AI said nuclear weapons. This is a weird
thing to do. I I think there was some
rational business reasons to do it. But
I think it was really unfortunate.
Right. As AI has taken off, we our
community convinced people not to trust
AI. Um and since then you know it stayed
at an elevated level of concern and one
of the contrast is this is a a median of
25 nations. Um we trust even though a
lot of AI was invented here in America
Americans trust or cons are or excited
about AI less than many other nations
and kind of this create headwinds. Uh
when we want to build a data center it
turns out one of the best things you can

computing and data centers. But when
people hear oh AI could lead to human

building data centers and this is a bad
for environment and bad for AI. So I
think we have work ahead of us to win
over heights and minds and bring people
along with us. Um the flip side just to
wrap up is uh the good news is people
actually do want AI. A few of my friends
Deg Eric and others stand there ran a
study where they took 10 jobs limited
scope study broke down to task and ran a
survey to ask how much do people want AI
to help automate task and it was
decently high 46%. And it turns out that
um it was kind of a one to five star
rating. It turns out a lot of tasks a
lot of people do that they want AI help
with uh tax preparers they like their
job but they don't like scheding client
appointments. So 911 operator I think
they like helping people but maintaining
call logs you know they they love AI to
help with that. So a lot of things that
people want AI want us to build stuff to
help them with the most common reasons
for wanting automation is maybe not
nothing that shocking frees up time for
high value work tasks repetitive
automation you know kind of this is this
is very very um sensible stuff that
people want AI to help with um and on
the flip side there also tasks that they
don't really want AI to help with. So
editors they like writing text they
don't want AI to automate writing text
but there was a lot of stuff 46% of the
task um considered in this study that we
could go and build and the set of tasks
is incredibly diverse. So just to wrap
up I think um with AI capabilities
there's so much that we can do that is
so powerful and that we can build so
fast. This is the best time I've ever
seen in my life and I think it only get
even better over time to build stuff and
there's a lot of stuff that people can
uh use a help to build. And maybe I I'll
just wrap with one note. I I'm I was
really excited to come here in person
and meet many of you. Just a fun story.
Um at the last AI dev conference that we

you know meeting people in person that

as we build stuff this community and
getting together to meet learn from each
other share ideas that is important so
in addition to hearing the presentations
I hope all of you will as Jonathan was
saying meet each other, make friends,
um, and form lasting connections.
Excited to meet many more of your AI
